text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY - INITIAL CONSULTATION NOTE

ATTENDING: [GU Oncology Attending]
DIVISION: Hematology/Oncology, Genitourinary Program

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Mr. Robert Delacroix is a 68-year-old French-Canadian male with newly diagnosed metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer presenting for initial oncology consultation and 3-month treatment follow-up visit following initiation of systemic therapy.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Mr. Delacroix is a 68-year-old retired civil engineer with a recent diagnosis of Stage IVB prostatic adenocarcinoma (cT3bN1M1b) who presents today approximately 3 months after initiating androgen deprivation therapy in combination with abiraterone acetate plus prednisone. His diagnosis was established after he presented with worsening lower urinary tract symptoms (nocturia x4-5, decreased urinary stream, incomplete bladder emptying) and new onset of right hip and lower back pain of approximately 6-8 weeks duration. Digital rectal exam at his urologist's office revealed a hard, irregular, markedly enlarged prostate gland with loss of the sulcus. PSA at the time of diagnosis was 87.4 ng/mL.

Subsequent workup included a 12-core transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy, which demonstrated high-grade acinar adenocarcinoma involving all 12 cores bilaterally. Gleason score was 9 (4+5), Grade Group 5. Immunohistochemistry was notable for PSA positivity and strong PSMA expression. Staging technetium-99m bone scan demonstrated extensive polyostotic skeletal metastatic disease including bilateral sacroiliac joints, L2-L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, right femoral neck and proximal shaft, and left posterior 7th rib. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast showed prostate gland enlarged at approximately 65 cc with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, pelvic lymphadenopathy (right obturator node 2.1 cm, left external iliac node 1.4 cm), and mixed lytic/sclerotic osseous lesions consistent with metastatic disease. No visceral metastases were identified at the time of staging.

Given the diagnosis of metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (mCSPC) with high-volume disease (bone metastases including axial and appendicular skeleton, pelvic nodal involvement), he was initiated on combined ADT and novel hormonal intensification approximately 3 months ago. He was started on leuprolide acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (first injection administered), combined with abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily with prednisone 5 mg twice daily. Bicalutamide 50 mg daily was used as a flare prophylaxis for 4 weeks at ADT initiation and has since been discontinued. A testosterone level obtained at 4 weeks confirmed castrate status at less than 20 ng/dL.

He returns today for his initial 3-month oncology follow-up. The patient reports that he has been compliant with his oral medications and his leuprolide injection was administered on schedule. He is tolerating the regimen reasonably well overall.

Symptom review specific to treatment:
- Fatigue: Present, grade 1. He describes mild tiredness by late afternoon but is still able to perform light activities around the house and accompany his wife on short walks.
- Hot flashes: Present, grade 2. He is experiencing 4-6 episodes daily, each lasting several minutes, occasionally disruptive to sleep. He was counseled regarding this expected side effect at initiation.
- Lower extremity edema: Bilateral, mild (grade 1), most pronounced in the ankles by end of day. He has been elevating his legs with partial relief.
- Libido: Markedly reduced, as expected. He and his wife have been counseled regarding sexual side effects of ADT.
- Urinary symptoms: Improved urinary flow compared to pre-treatment. Nocturia reduced to approximately 1-2 times per night.
- Back pain: The right hip and lumbar pain that prompted initial evaluation is substantially improved. He is using acetaminophen 500 mg PRN, currently about 2-3 times per week.
- No nausea, vomiting, or significant gastrointestinal symptoms.
- Mood: Reports mild mood changes and occasional irritability, which he attributes to hormonal changes. No depressed mood meeting clinical threshold, denies suicidal ideation.
- Cognitive: Reports mild cognitive dulling, though not significantly impacting daily function.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
Constitutional: Fatigue as above, no fever, no significant unintentional weight loss (weight stable per patient report).
HEENT: No headache, no visual changes.
Cardiovascular: Mild lower extremity edema. No chest pain, no exertional dyspnea, no palpitations.
Respiratory: No cough, no shortness of breath.
Gastrointestinal: Appetite intact. No nausea. Mild constipation noted, managed with dietary fiber. No rectal bleeding.
Genitourinary: Improved LUTS. Minimal hematuria. Erectile dysfunction, expected on ADT.
Musculoskeletal: Residual mild right hip discomfort, improved on acetaminophen. No new bone pain.
Neurological: No radicular symptoms. No extremity weakness or numbness. No bowel/bladder dysfunction.
Endocrine: Hot flashes as above.
Psychiatric: Mild emotional lability, no active depression or anxiety disorder identified.
Dermatologic: No rash. No injection site reaction at leuprolide site.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY:
1. Metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (cT3bN1M1b, Gleason 9, diagnosed approximately 3 months ago) - as detailed above
2. Hypertension - well-controlled on current medications
3. Hyperlipidemia - on statin therapy
4. Benign prostatic hyperplasia (pre-existing, now subsumed by malignant diagnosis)
5. Osteopenia - documented on DEXA scan performed at time of ADT initiation (T-score -1.7 lumbar spine); note metastatic skeletal involvement complicates bone density interpretation
6. No prior cardiac events; stress testing performed prior to initiating abiraterone - no significant ischemic changes
7. No prior history of other malignancies
8. No prior abdominal or pelvic surgeries

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY:
- None relevant

FAMILY HISTORY:
- Father: Prostate cancer, diagnosed in his 70s, died age 78 (cause of death prostate cancer)
- Paternal aunt: Breast cancer, diagnosed age 62
- No known family history of ovarian, pancreatic, or colorectal cancers
- Family history raises concern for possible hereditary cancer syndrome (e.g., BRCA2-associated); germline genetic testing has been ordered and results pending

SOCIAL HISTORY:
- Retired civil engineer, previously worked in municipal infrastructure
- Lives at home with his wife of 43 years; two adult children
- Former smoker: approximately 30 pack-year history, quit at age 55
- Alcohol: Non-drinker
- No recreational drug use
- Performance status: ECOG 1
- Good social support; wife attends all appointments
- French-Canadian descent

ALLERGIES:
- No known drug allergies
- No known food allergies

MEDICATIONS:
1. Leuprolide acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months - last injection today
2. Abiraterone acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on an empty stomach
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (with abiraterone)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily (hypertension)
5. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
6. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (bone pain)
7. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally twice daily (constipation prophylaxis)
8. Bicalutamide 50 mg daily - DISCONTINUED (completed 4-week flare prophylaxis course)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vitals: Blood pressure 132/78 mmHg, Heart rate 68 bpm, Respiratory rate 16 breaths/min, Temperature 98.3°F (36.8°C), O2 saturation 97% on room air, Weight 89 kg, Height 176 cm, BMI 28.7 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational, appropriate affect.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis. No lymphadenopathy appreciated in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or dullness to percussion.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly on palpation. No masses appreciated.

Genitourinary: Deferred formal DRE given known diagnosis and clinical context.

Musculoskeletal: Mild tenderness to palpation over right sacroiliac region and right femoral neck on deep palpation. Ambulatory without assistance. Gait normal.

Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in all extremities. No focal deficits. No signs of cord compression (lower extremity reflexes normal bilaterally, no clonus, normal rectal tone reported by patient).

Extremities: Bilateral 1+ pitting edema to the level of the ankles. No erythema, warmth, or asymmetry to suggest DVT.

Skin: No rash, no jaundice, no pallor.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS:
PSA: 3.2 ng/mL (baseline at diagnosis: 87.4 ng/mL; 96.3% decline)
Serum testosterone: 14 ng/dL (confirmed castrate; goal < 50 ng/dL)
CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 12.9 g/dL, Platelets 224 x10^9/L
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139 mEq/L, Potassium 4.2 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR 72 mL/min/1.73m2), BUN 18 mg/dL
Liver function tests: ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, Alkaline phosphatase 148 U/L (mildly elevated, consistent with known osseous metastatic disease)
Albumin: 3.9 g/dL
LDH: 184 U/L
Blood glucose (fasting): 102 mg/dL (borderline, attributed in part to prednisone)
HbA1c: 5.8% (pre-diabetic range, monitoring warranted given corticosteroid use)
Mineralocorticoid-related labs: Blood pressure as above, potassium normal; no signs of mineralocorticoid excess
PTH and vitamin D 25-OH level: 25-OH vitamin D 22 ng/mL (insufficient; will supplement), PTH 62 pg/mL (borderline elevated, consistent with low vitamin D)
Germline genetic testing: PENDING (sent; results expected within 2-3 weeks)

---

IMAGING RESULTS:
Prior technetium-99m bone scan (staging): Multiple areas of increased radiotracer uptake consistent with osseous metastatic disease involving bilateral sacroiliac joints, L2, L3, L4 vertebral bodies, right iliac wing, right femoral neck and proximal shaft, and left posterior 7th rib. Extensive polyostotic skeletal involvement.

Prior CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (staging): Enlarged heterogeneous prostate with bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, pelvic nodal disease, mixed lytic/sclerotic osseous lesions. No visceral metastases at time of staging.

No repeat imaging performed at this visit; repeat bone scan and/or CT anticipated at 6-9 months per clinical course.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

Mr. Delacroix is a 68-year-old gentleman with newly diagnosed Stage IVB metastatic castration-sensitive prostate cancer (cT3bN1M1b, Gleason 9 Grade Group 5, high-volume disease by CHAARTED criteria) now 3 months into combined ADT plus abiraterone/prednisone (Zytiga/prednisone) intensification therapy. He is demonstrating an excellent early biochemical response with PSA declining from 87.4 to 3.2 ng/mL, a 96% reduction, with confirmed castrate testosterone. Treatment is well-tolerated with expected side effects.

1. METASTATIC CASTRATION-SENSITIVE PROSTATE CANCER - RESPONDING TO THERAPY
   - Excellent PSA response as above (96% decline from baseline), with confirmed castrate testosterone at 14 ng/dL
   - Continue leuprolide 22.5 mg IM q3 months - second injection administered today
   - Continue abiraterone (Zytiga) 1000 mg daily on empty stomach with prednisone 5 mg BID
   - Target PSA nadir anticipated over next 3-6 months; will continue to monitor
   - Germline NGS results pending - will review at next visit; implications for HRR pathway and PARP inhibitor eligibility if applicable
   - Repeat PSA and testosterone in 8 weeks
   - Repeat bone scan and CT CAP at approximately 6-month mark from therapy initiation, or sooner if clinical concern arises

2. BONE METASTASES / SKELETAL-RELATED EVENT PREVENTION
   - Initiating zoledronic acid (Zometa) 4 mg IV every 3 months for bone-directed therapy given high burden of osseous disease and ongoing ADT-related bone density loss
   - Renal function adequate (creatinine 1.0, eGFR 72) - standard dose appropriate; will recheck creatinine prior to each infusion
   - Baseline dental evaluation ordered prior to first zoledronic acid infusion (osteonecrosis of jaw prophylaxis)
   - Start calcium carbonate 1200 mg orally daily with food
   - Start vitamin D3 1000 IU orally daily (25-OH vitamin D low at 22 ng/mL; target > 30 ng/mL)
   - Patient instructed on signs/symptoms of skeletal complications (new severe bone pain, neurologic changes, fracture)

3. TREATMENT-RELATED TOXICITIES
   - Fatigue (grade 1): Expected on ADT + abiraterone; counseled on energy conservation, light aerobic exercise as tolerated. Will refer to oncology rehabilitation if worsens.
   - Hot flashes (grade 2): Counseled on behavioral measures (loose clothing, fans, cold water). Patient declines pharmacologic intervention at this time; will revisit if worsening.
   - Lower extremity edema (grade 1): Counseled on leg elevation, compression stockings. Edema likely multifactorial (abiraterone-related sodium retention + decreased mobility). Monitor blood pressure closely; no dose reduction warranted at this time.
   - Borderline fasting glucose/pre-diabetic HbA1c: Attributed in part to prednisone. Dietary counseling. Will monitor fasting glucose with each lab draw. Endocrinology referral deferred for now; threshold for referral is development of frank hyperglycemia.
   - Osteopenia (pre-existing): DEXA performed at ADT initiation; T-score -1.7 lumbar spine. Calcium and vitamin D as above; zoledronic acid will serve dual purpose.

4. HYPERTENSION / CARDIOVASCULAR MONITORING
   - Blood pressure 132/78 today, well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg
   - Abiraterone can cause mineralocorticoid excess (hypokalemia, hypertension, fluid retention) - mitigated by concurrent prednisone; potassium normal today
   - Continue lisinopril; will monitor BP and electrolytes at each visit
   - Atorvastatin continued for cardiovascular risk management

5. GENETIC COUNSELING
   - Germline testing ordered given family history (father with prostate cancer, paternal aunt with breast cancer)
   - Results pending; once available, will review with patient and refer for formal genetic counseling
   - If germline HRR mutation identified, implications for family members and for future treatment selection will be discussed

6. SUPPORTIVE CARE / QUALITY OF LIFE
   - Encouraged ongoing physical activity as tolerated
   - Social work referral offered; patient deferred at this time but may revisit
   - Prescription for compression stockings provided
   - Continue acetaminophen PRN for bone pain; counseled on acetaminophen dosing limits given prednisone use

RETURN TO CLINIC: 8 weeks for PSA, testosterone, CMP, CBC; sooner PRN for acute concerns.

Time with patient: 45 minutes. Greater than 50% of visit time spent in counseling and coordination of care.

[Dictated by GU Oncology Attending]
